title: Severe Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome
chief_complaint: I did IVF and had my egg retrieval 5 days ago. Now my belly is huge and painful, I can't breathe well, I've gained 10 pounds in 3 days, and I'm barely peeing. I'm nauseous and can't keep anything down.

physical_exam.Abdomen: Severely distended, tense, shifting dullness (ascites). Tenderness diffuse. No rebound.
physical_exam.Lungs: Decreased breath sounds bibasilar (pleural effusions). Dullness to percussion.
physical_exam.Pelvic: Enlarged ovaries (12cm each - massively enlarged). Tender. No adnexal torsion on US (doppler flow present).
physical_exam.Hydration: Dry mucous membranes. Poor skin turgor. Oliguria.

hidden_data.ob_us: Bilateral massively enlarged ovaries (Right 14cm, Left 12cm) with multiple cysts and ascites. No ovarian torsion.
hidden_data.cbc: Hb 16.5 (hemoconcentration), WBC 22,000, Platelets 300,000. Hct 52% (severe hemoconcentration - >45% is severe).
hidden_data.cmp: Creatinine 2.2 (prerenal AKI), BUN 45, Na 130 (dilutional hyponatremia), K+ 5.5, ALT 150, AST 180. Total protein 5.0 (low - protein loss into ascites).
hidden_data.ascitic_fluid: Transudate (SAAG <1.1 - from increased vascular permeability). Negative for infection.

correct_diagnosis: Severe Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome (OHSS) - Critical Grade with Ascites, Pleural Effusions, Renal Failure, and Hemoconcentration
key_learning_points: OHSS = excessive ovarian response to gonadotropin stimulation. Most common iatrogenic complication of IVF. Pathophysiology: hCG (exogenous or endogenous from pregnancy) → VEGF release from hyperstimulated ovaries → increased vascular permeability → fluid shifts from intravascular to third space (ascites, pleural effusions) → intravascular depletion → hemoconcentration, renal failure, thromboembolism

Risk factors: Young age (<35), Low BMI, PCOS (high antral follicle count), High AMH, Previous OHSS, High estradiol (>5,000), Large number of follicles (>20), hCG trigger (vs GnRH agonist trigger - which eliminates OHSS risk). Pregnancy increases severity and duration (endogenous hCG)

Classification: Mild: Abdominal distension, ovarian <8cm. Moderate: Nausea/vomiting, US ascites, ovarian 8-12cm. Severe: Clinical ascites, oliguria, Hct >45%, Cr >1.5, Liver dysfunction, Ovaries >12cm. Critical: Tense ascites, Hct >55%, Cr >2, Thromboembolism, ARDS

Management: SUPPORTIVE. Most cases self-limited (resolve within 7-10 days if not pregnant, longer if pregnant). 1) IV fluids: NS or LR (restore intravascular volume, improve renal perfusion). 2) Pain control: Acetaminophen (avoid NSAIDs - worsen renal function). 3) Antiemetics: Ondansetron IV. 4) Paracentesis: US-guided for tense ascites causing respiratory compromise or oliguria. Drain slowly (risk of hypotension). 5) Thoracentesis: For symptomatic pleural effusions. 6) Thromboprophylaxis: LMWH (Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily) - OHSS patients are hypercoagulable. 7) ICU if critical. 8) Dopamine agonists (Cabergoline 0.5mg daily ×8 days) - inhibits VEGF receptor phosphorylation, reduces OHSS severity (preventive). 9) Discontinue any exogenous hCG support

Prevention: GnRH antagonist protocol (allows GnRH agonist trigger). GnRH agonist trigger (Leuprolide) instead of hCG - completely eliminates OHSS risk (works by inducing endogenous LH surge which is shorter duration than hCG). 'Freeze-all' strategy: Cryopreserve all embryos, no fresh transfer (avoids pregnancy → no endogenous hCG). Cabergoline after retrieval. Coasting (withhold gonadotropins until estradiol drops). Metformin in PCOS patients
